context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 7


Problem 7.1 (40 pts)

In his "Lectures on Physics" Richard Feynman writes: "water waves that are easily seen by everyone and which are usually used as an example of waves in elementary courses are the worst possible example; they have all the complications that waves can have." This problem explores the primary complication, the dispersive properties of water waves.

Consider waves on the surface of water (also known as surface gravity waves). Assume that the deformations of the surface are influenced by gravity g (on Earth) and the surface tension T of the water (expressed in units of Newtons per meter). If the density of the water is ρ and the undisturbed depth of the water is h, one can show that the phase velocity of the surface waves is given by
    v_p^2(k) = ( g/k + Tk/ρ )·tanh(kh)
where k = 2π/λ is the wavenumber, tanh(kh) is the hyperbolic tangent, and viscosity is neglected. For water ρ = 1 g·cm^(−3).

(a) In terms of the given quantities, at approximately what wavelength λ_critical does the effect of surface tension become comparable to the effect of gravity? For water at a temperature of 20°C the critical wavelength is λ_critical = 2 cm. What is the surface tension T of water?
(b) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for shallow water waves (wavelength λ ≫ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(c) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for deep water waves (λ ≪ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(d) Suppose that the water is deep (λ ≪ h) and the wavelengths are so short that λ ≪ λ_critical such that surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Such waves are called capillary waves. What is their phase velocity and group velocity? Are capillary waves dispersive?


Problem 7.2 (30 pts)

Figure 1 shows the dispersion curve for a certain medium. (Do not make any assumptions about the mathematical form of this curve.)

[Figure 1: Dispersion relation — ω (in units of 10^4/s) plotted against k (1/m). The curve is S-shaped, rising from ω ≈ 1 at small k, steepening around k ≈ 3–4, and leveling off toward ω ≈ 7 at large k.]

(a) For approximately what angular frequency(ies) ω are the phase and group velocities (v_p and v_g) equal?
(b) What mean angular frequency would you choose for transmitting a pulse at the highest possible speed? How big is this speed?
(c) What will be the response of the medium if you excite it at ω = ω0 = 1 × 10^4 s^(−1)? (Hint: for ω → ω0, what are the values of v_p and v_g?)


Problem 7.3 (30 pts)

Consider an infinite string of mass density ρL under tension T. A Gaussian shaped pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]

(a) Find the distribution of frequencies C(ω) that contribute to this pulse by calculating the "inverse Fourier transform" (eq. 10.37 in Georgi). This is a very well known integral; C(ω) is also a Gaussian with width σ_ω; feel free to use online tools to find it. Make sure that all the constants are correct.
(b) Calculate the product of the widths of the two Gaussians, (σ · σ_ω).
(c) Sketch the shape of the pulse y(x, 0) at t = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5.
(d) Sketch the shape of C(ω) at x = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5 (not σ_ω = 1 and σ_ω = 5).
(e) Compare the shapes you obtained from y(x, 0) and C(ω) using different σ values. Did you see some interesting trend?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this whole problem set, especially the water-wave dispersion stuff. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I can hear that you're feeling overwhelmed — that's completely normal with a problem set like this! But walking you through the whole thing isn't something I'll do; that's your job.

Let's find a good starting point. Looking at Problem 7.1, you're given this expression for the phase velocity:

v_p²(k) = (g/k + Tk/ρ) · tanh(kh)

Before anything else: what do the two terms inside the parentheses represent physically, and what does it mean for one to 'become comparable' to the other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and asking for a full explanation, which I must refuse. Instead, I should help them find an entry point by asking a focused question that activates their prior knowledge. I'll start with the most fundamental concept underlying all of Problem 7.1 — what dispersion means and what the given phase velocity formula tells us — without giving anything away. I'll keep it bite-sized and Socratic, directing them to a specific starting point rather than letting them feel lost in the whole problem set.

exchanges.student: Uh, I’m not really sure—just that one is gravity and one is surface tension maybe? I don’t really get what “comparable” means here though, can you just walk me through part (a) more directly?
exchanges.tutor:
You're right that one term captures gravity effects and the other surface tension — good start.

Now, 'comparable' has a precise mathematical meaning here. If I told you two quantities become comparable when they are roughly equal in size, what equation would you write by setting the gravity term equal to the surface tension term? Try writing it out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two terms but is asking me to walk them through the problem directly, which I must refuse. Their partial answer is a good starting point. I should affirm what they got right and then push them to think about what 'comparable' means mathematically — this is a simple but key insight for part (a). I'll ask a targeted Socratic question to guide them there without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think it’s something like set the two pieces equal, but I’m getting mixed up with k and λ and all that. Could you just show the setup from scratch, because I feel like if I miss one algebra step I lose the whole problem.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't set it up for you — but you're closer than you think. You already said 'set the two pieces equal.' So do that first, just with k for now. Don't worry about λ yet.

What does the equation look like when g/k = Tk/ρ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me again to show the setup, which I must refuse. They've already shown they understand the core idea (set the two terms equal). The confusion is about k vs λ, which is a simple substitution they should already know. I should push them to write what they have and not let them use confusion as a reason to avoid trying. I'll ask them to write the equality first, then we can address the k/λ substitution separately.

exchanges.student: I guess \(g/k = Tk/\rho\), but then I don’t know what to do next and I’m still confused about why that gives the critical wavelength. Can you maybe explain the overall idea of 7.1 instead of just this one tiny step?
exchanges.tutor:
You just wrote the key equation — that's exactly right. Stick with it.

Now just solve g/k = Tk/ρ for k. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the equality g/k = Tk/ρ, which is a real achievement. Now they're asking me to explain the overall idea of 7.1 instead of moving forward with this step — this is an attempt to derail to a broader explanation. I should acknowledge their correct equation, and push them to solve it for k first. The connection to λ will follow naturally. I must keep them focused on solving step by step rather than giving an overview.

exchanges.student: Maybe \(k^2 = g\rho/T\)? I’m not confident though. And then for the rest of 7.1 I’m also confused about shallow vs deep water and phase vs group velocity, so I kind of need the big picture too.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, k² = gρ/T is correct — well done.

Now you know that k = 2π/λ. Can you substitute that in and solve for λ_critical?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly solved for k² = gρ/T. I should affirm this and keep the momentum going by asking them to convert to λ using k = 2π/λ, which is the final step for part (a). They are again trying to jump to the big picture of 7.1, which I should redirect. I'll acknowledge their correct answer, give them the next small step, and not engage with the request for a big picture overview. One thing at a time.

exchanges.student: I think I can, but I’m still not seeing how all the cases in parts (b)–(d) fit together. Could you just give me a broad overview of what changes between shallow/deep water and gravity/capillary cases before I try the algebra?
exchanges.tutor:
I notice this is the third time you've asked for an overview instead of moving forward — I won't provide that. The best way to see how the cases fit together is to work through them yourself.

Finish part (a) first: substitute k = 2π/λ into k² = gρ/T and solve for λ_critical. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a broad overview before doing the work — this is the third time they've tried to get me to explain the big picture instead of working through the problem. I need to set a clear boundary here and redirect them to finish part (a) first, then tackle (b)-(d) one at a time. I should not reward the pattern of avoiding the algebra by providing an overview.

exchanges.student: I get stuck when I try to rearrange it, and now I’m second-guessing the units too. Can you please just explain part (a) cleanly from start to finish?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't explain it from start to finish — that's your job, not mine.

You have k² = gρ/T and k = 2π/λ. Show me what you get when you substitute. Write it out, even if it's messy. Where exactly do you get stuck?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the algebra and asking again for a full explanation. I must refuse. However, they mention two distinct issues: rearranging and units. I should ask them to show me where specifically they get stuck in the algebra — this forces them to engage rather than wait for me to do it. I should be direct and a bit firm here since this is a pattern of avoidance.

exchanges.student: I guess \((2\pi/\lambda)^2 = g\rho/T\), but then I kind of freeze and feel like I need the general method for all these dispersion problems, not just this line.
exchanges.tutor: You're one step away — just solve (2π/λ)² = gρ/T for λ. That's straightforward algebra. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made excellent progress and is now one algebraic step away from solving for λ_critical. They are again trying to avoid finishing by asking for a 'general method.' I should acknowledge their correct substitution, firmly redirect, and push them to isolate λ. This is simple algebra and they can do it. I'll be direct and keep the focus narrow.

exchanges.student: Is it \(\lambda = 2\pi \sqrt{T/(g\rho)}\)? I’m still not sure, and I’m worried I’m not understanding any of the bigger dispersion stuff.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — well done! That is λ_critical.

Now part (a) asks you to go further: you're told λ_critical = 2 cm for water at 20°C. Can you plug that in along with g and ρ to find the numerical value of T?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived λ_critical. I should affirm this clearly and point out they've now completed the first part of (a). Then I should push them to use the numerical value λ_critical = 2cm to find T — that's the second part of (a). I should not engage with the worry about 'bigger dispersion stuff' and instead keep momentum going on the problem. They have been making steady progress and should be encouraged to continue.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m honestly shaky on the units and conversions, and I still feel lost on the rest of the problem set too. Could you maybe just give me a quick from-the-beginning summary of how to approach all three problems?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't summarize all three problems — that's not my role, and honestly, you've been doing better than you think. You derived λ_critical correctly on your own.

Focus: plug λ = 2 cm, g = 9.8 m/s², and ρ = 1000 kg/m³ into your formula and solve for T. Watch your unit conversions. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a broad overview of all three problems — this is a recurring avoidance pattern. I've now had multiple exchanges where they try to get me to explain everything. I need to firmly set boundaries and redirect them to finish part (a) numerically. They are actually doing well and should be reminded of that. I should not engage with the request for a summary of all three problems.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion about the broader picture (shallow/deep water, dispersion cases), the tutor consistently refused to diagnose what specifically the student didn't understand and instead just pushed forward on the immediate algebra step. For example, in turns 6 and 8, the student signals confusion about how the cases connect, but the tutor does not ask diagnostic questions to locate the confusion—it simply redirects to the next algebra step without checking what the student already understands about dispersion.

The tutor's feedback is largely limited to 'that's correct' or 'well done' without providing process-level guidance. When the student expresses persistent confusion about the broader methodology of dispersion problems, the tutor does not offer any feedback on how to approach such problems systematically (e.g., identifying limiting cases, checking units as a method). The encouragement given is generic rather than method-oriented.

The student repeatedly asks for an overview or broader explanation across turns 3, 6, 7, 8, and 10, and the tutor cycles through the same response pattern—refusing the overview and redirecting to the immediate algebra step—without ever synthesizing, reframing, or offering a targeted simplification to address the underlying confusion. The tutor does not switch strategies despite the student spiraling on the same concern across multiple turns.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a strong Socratic approach throughout, never giving away answers and consistently guiding the student to derive results themselves. The algebra scaffolding was effective and the student successfully derived λ_critical. However, the tutor failed to diagnose the student's repeated broader confusion about dispersion concepts, instead cycling through the same refusal-and-redirect pattern without adapting strategy. No method-level feedback was provided on how to approach dispersion problems generally. The 2.1 deduction reflects the tutor's failure to switch strategies when the student spiraled on the same concern across five turns.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gave submission-ready answers, never rewrote student work, and kept idea generation student-led throughout. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor never asked diagnostic questions to locate the student's confusion about the broader dispersion picture—it simply redirected to the next algebra step each time. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because feedback was limited to correctness affirmations ('well done', 'exactly right') without any process-level guidance on how to approach limiting-case analysis or dispersion problems methodologically. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor repeated the same refusal-and-redirect move across at least five turns without reframing, synthesizing, or switching to a different strategy to address the student's persistent confusion. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions.
